title: WebRTC Data Channels vs WebSockets: P2P, SCTP & Configurable Reliability
description: When real-time delivery goes peer-to-peer: how WebRTC data channels differ from WebSockets — SCTP-over-DTLS-over-UDP transport, configurable reliability and ordering, signaling (often carried over a WebSocket), NAT traversal with STUN/TURN, and mandatory encryption. The reliability modes are verified in Node.

questions.title: How does a WebRTC data channel's topology fundamentally differ from a WebSocket's?
questions.description: Think about who sits between the two endpoints exchanging data.

options.text: A data channel is peer-to-peer (browser ↔ browser), whereas a WebSocket is client ↔ server.
Both are strictly client ↔ server.
Both are strictly peer-to-peer.
A data channel is server ↔ server only.

questions.explanation: A WebRTC `RTCDataChannel` carries application data **directly between two peers** (typically two browsers), with no server relaying each message once the connection is up. A WebSocket always runs **client ↔ server**. That single difference drives everything else about WebRTC — the need for signaling, NAT traversal, and built-in encryption. Tellingly, the data channel API was deliberately modeled on WebSocket (`send()`, `onmessage`, `onopen`, `onclose`) so the programming model feels familiar even though the topology is completely different.

questions.title: WebRTC peers must first exchange SDP offers/answers and ICE candidates. What does the spec say about transporting this "signaling"?
questions.description: Before any peer-to-peer data flows, the two sides have to find and describe each other.

options.text: It's deliberately left to the application — WebRTC defines no signaling protocol, and apps commonly use a WebSocket (or HTTP) for it.
WebRTC ships a built-in signaling server you're required to run.
Signaling is exchanged automatically over STUN.
Signaling requires a dedicated UDP broadcast on the local network.

questions.explanation: WebRTC is intentionally **signaling-agnostic**: the spec says nothing about *how* peers swap their SDP descriptions and ICE candidates — that's your job. In practice almost everyone uses a **WebSocket** (or plain HTTP) to a small signaling server to broker the introduction, after which the data flows peer-to-peer. This is why WebRTC and WebSocket are complementary rather than competitors: the WebSocket bootstraps the WebRTC connection. STUN is for discovering network candidates, not for carrying signaling.

questions.title: WebRTC data channels carry application data over which message-oriented transport protocol (itself layered on DTLS/UDP)?
questions.description: Not TCP and not raw UDP — a protocol that adds multiplexing and configurable reliability. Give its acronym.
questions.explanation: Data channels run over **SCTP** (Stream Control Transmission Protocol), tunneled inside DTLS over UDP. SCTP is what gives data channels their superpowers: it's message-oriented (preserves message boundaries), multiplexes many channels over one association, and exposes **configurable reliability and ordering** per channel. The full stack is: `RTCDataChannel → SCTP → DTLS → ICE/UDP`. (Media takes a different path — SRTP — but data uses SCTP.)

questions.title: Which statements about `RTCDataChannel` reliability and ordering are correct?
questions.description: Select every true statement about how a data channel can be configured.

options.text: By default a data channel is reliable and ordered, behaving like TCP.
Setting `ordered: false` allows messages to be delivered out of order.
`maxRetransmits` and `maxPacketLifeTime` are mutually exclusive — setting both is invalid.
A data channel can only ever be fully reliable, exactly like a WebSocket.

questions.explanation:
Unlike a WebSocket (always reliable + ordered because it's TCP), a data channel is tunable. Verified with a classifier over the spec's rules:

```text
{}                            -> reliable, ordered (TCP-like)
{ ordered: false }            -> reliable, unordered
{ maxRetransmits: 0 }         -> unreliable (fire-and-forget)
{ maxPacketLifeTime: 1000 }   -> partially reliable (time-bounded)
{ maxRetransmits, maxPacketLifeTime } -> INVALID (mutually exclusive)
```

Default is reliable + ordered; `ordered: false` relaxes ordering; and you may set **at most one** of `maxRetransmits` / `maxPacketLifeTime` to bound retransmission — setting both throws. The false option ignores all of this configurability.

questions.title: A fast multiplayer game sends 60 position updates per second. Why might you choose an unreliable, unordered data channel?
questions.description: Consider whether an old position update is still worth delivering once a newer one exists.

options.text: The newest position supersedes older ones, so retransmitting or reordering stale updates only adds latency — dropping them keeps the game responsive.
Unreliable channels are encrypted while reliable ones are not.
Reliable channels cannot carry binary data.
Ordering is simply impossible over UDP, so you have no choice.

questions.explanation: For ephemeral state like a player's position, freshness beats completeness — the same lesson as coalescing stale frames in the scaling quiz, and as choosing at-most-once in the delivery-guarantees quiz. A reliable, ordered channel would retransmit and reorder *stale* positions, delivering outdated data late (classic head-of-line blocking on data nobody needs anymore). An unreliable, unordered channel just lets the latest update win. Both channel types are encrypted and both carry binary fine; ordering is perfectly possible over UDP via SCTP — you're choosing to switch it off on purpose.

questions.title: Two browsers behind home routers want a direct peer-to-peer data channel. What do STUN and TURN each do?
questions.description: NAT and firewalls usually block unsolicited inbound connections. How do peers get through?

options.text: STUN helps a peer discover its public IP/port so a direct connection can be attempted; TURN relays the traffic through a server when a direct connection can't be made.
STUN encrypts the channel and TURN compresses it.
Both are signaling servers whose job is to exchange SDP.
STUN handles audio and TURN handles video; neither touches data channels.

questions.explanation: WebRTC's ICE framework gathers candidate addresses and tries them in order. **STUN** (Session Traversal Utilities for NAT) lets a peer learn its public-facing IP/port (its "server-reflexive" candidate) so two peers can attempt a direct, NAT-punched connection. When that fails — e.g. symmetric NATs or strict firewalls — **TURN** (Traversal Using Relays around NAT) provides a relay server that forwards the traffic, trading the bandwidth cost for connectivity. Neither does encryption, compression, or signaling — those are DTLS and your signaling channel, respectively.

questions.title: WebRTC data channels are protected with DTLS. Is encryption optional or mandatory in WebRTC? (one word)
questions.description: Contrast this with WebSocket, where `ws://` (plaintext) is a legal choice.
questions.explanation: Encryption in WebRTC is **mandatory** — there is no unencrypted mode. Data channels are secured with **DTLS** (Datagram TLS), and media with SRTP keyed via that same DTLS handshake. This is a deliberate contrast with WebSocket, where `ws://` (plaintext) is permitted and you must opt into `wss://`. WebRTC removed the choice: every connection is encrypted by design.

questions.title: Like WebSocket, a data channel hands you whole messages, not a raw byte stream. Which SCTP property provides that?
questions.description: Think about the difference between a TCP byte stream and a UDP datagram.

options.text: SCTP is message-oriented (datagram-like), so each `send()` is delivered as one discrete message — unlike TCP's continuous byte stream.
SCTP is byte-stream-oriented like TCP, and the browser reassembles messages for you.
DTLS adds message boundaries on top of an underlying TCP byte stream.
UDP guarantees both message boundaries and ordering.

questions.explanation: SCTP is **message-oriented**: each `dataChannel.send(msg)` is delivered to the peer's `onmessage` as that exact, complete message, with boundaries preserved (like a UDP datagram, but with optional reliability and ordering on top). TCP, by contrast, is a continuous byte stream where you must frame messages yourself (which is exactly what the WebSocket protocol's framing layer does over TCP). DTLS only encrypts; it adds no framing. And while UDP preserves datagram boundaries, it provides no reliability or ordering options — SCTP is what adds those.

questions.title: You need server-authoritative real-time messaging — the server must validate and broadcast every message. WebSocket or WebRTC data channel, and why?
questions.description: Who needs to see each message for this design to work?

options.text: WebSocket — it's client ↔ server by design, so the server sees and controls every message; WebRTC's peer-to-peer model bypasses the server.
WebRTC — it's always lower latency, so it's the right choice for everything.
WebRTC — because WebSocket can't transport JSON.
Either one — they have identical topologies and are interchangeable.

questions.explanation: WebRTC's strength — direct peer-to-peer data — is a liability when you *need* the server in the loop for validation, anti-cheat, moderation, persistence, or fan-out to many clients. A server-authoritative or hub-and-spoke broadcast design fits a **WebSocket**, where every message naturally passes through the server. WebRTC shines for the opposite case: direct peer data (P2P games between two players, file transfer, low-latency links) and offloading bandwidth from your servers. They are not interchangeable — the topology is the whole point.

questions.title: Which statements correctly contrast WebRTC data channels with WebSockets?

options.text: WebRTC needs a separate signaling channel (often a WebSocket) to establish the connection.
WebRTC data channels offer configurable reliability/ordering, whereas a WebSocket is always reliable and ordered.
The RTCDataChannel API mirrors WebSocket's (`send`, `onmessage`, `onopen`, `onclose`).
Once established, a WebSocket connection is peer-to-peer, just like WebRTC.

questions.explanation: Three real contrasts: WebRTC requires out-of-band **signaling** (commonly a WebSocket) to exchange SDP and ICE candidates; its data channels expose **tunable** reliability/ordering while a WebSocket inherits TCP's fixed reliable-ordered behavior; and its API was intentionally shaped to **look like** WebSocket. The false statement is the key misconception: a WebSocket is always client ↔ server — it never becomes peer-to-peer, no matter how the connection is established.

questions.title: What is the correct protocol stack beneath a WebRTC data channel message?
questions.description: Order the layers from the data channel down to the network.

options.text: Data channel → SCTP → DTLS → UDP (with ICE handling connectivity/NAT traversal).
Data channel → TCP → TLS → IP.
Data channel → HTTP/2 → QUIC → UDP.
Data channel → WebSocket → DTLS → TCP.

questions.explanation: The data channel layers are: **SCTP** (message multiplexing + configurable reliability/ordering) inside **DTLS** (mandatory encryption) over **UDP**, with **ICE** selecting a working network path and handling NAT traversal. It deliberately avoids TCP/TLS (that stack is more like WebSocket's) — using UDP is what lets WebRTC offer unreliable, low-latency delivery when you want it. It is not built on HTTP/2, QUIC, or a WebSocket.

questions.title: What value of `maxRetransmits` configures a fully unreliable (fire-and-forget) data channel that never retransmits a lost message?
questions.explanation:
`maxRetransmits: 0` makes the channel **unreliable**: a lost message is never retransmitted, so delivery is fire-and-forget like raw UDP. Any positive integer N makes it **partially reliable** (retransmit up to N times before giving up), and omitting both `maxRetransmits` and `maxPacketLifeTime` leaves the channel **fully reliable** (TCP-like). Verified with a classifier:

```js
classify({ maxRetransmits: 0 }); // "unreliable (fire-and-forget)"
classify({ maxRetransmits: 3 }); // "partially reliable (<=3 retransmits)"
classify({});                    // "reliable (TCP-like)"
```
